item.title: Overcoming barriers to point-of-care nucleic acid amplification testing
item.description:
Wednesday, July 1, 2026 at 6 PM

Location: BioScience Research Collaborative

In this thesis I address aspects of three key bottlenecks limiting clinical translation of isothermal nucleic acid amplification tests (isothermal NAATs) – (1) sample preparation, (2) assay integration and clinical validation, and (3) implementation analysis in real-world use environments. Addressing these bottlenecks is important to increase clinical access to highly sensitive and specific nucleic acid diagnostics where laboratory testing is unavailable. Currently, most people globally cannot access NAAT tests when they are needed. Isothermal NAATs, which operate at a single temperature reducing instrumentation requirements and processing time, have been developed to address this need. Despite substantial research and development on isothermal NAATs for many indications, very few have achieved widespread clinical adoption. To address the need for simple sample preparation suitable to the point-of-care, I focused on extraction-free sample preparation and developed a framework to guide the design and validation of extraction-free sample preparation methods based on gaps identified in the literature (Chapter 2 and 3). This framework focuses on identifying potential amplification inhibitors, determining the lytic efficiency of different methods, and creating appropriate model samples. This framework can be used by test developers to co-design an amplification assay and suitable extraction-free sample preparation workflow. When I then applied this framework in Chapter 3 to design an extraction-free sample preparation workflow for an assay detecting β-globin from buccal swabs and blood, I was able to design extraction-free sample preparation that consistently detected target from both buccal swabs and blood. I then applied the framework I developed in Chapters 4 and 5 to optimize extraction-free sample preparation for assays for two indications. In Chapter 4, to address the need for assay integration and clinical validation, I optimized SCD-RPA, an isothermal NAAT to diagnose sickle cell anemia and sickle cell trait. I integrated the assay with a modified version of the previously developed sample preparation workflow and validated it in a multi-country study with 161 patient samples collected in both a high- and a low-resource setting. SCD-RPA demonstrated robust performance with an overall genotype prediction accuracy of 96.9%. In Chapter 5, I applied the framework for extraction-free sample preparation to HPV-LAMP, a test being developed for cervical cancer screening, and achieved good analytical performance (30.4 HPV16 positive cells, 7.6 HPV18 positive cells, andhttps://riceuniversity.zoom.us/j/94882434656?pwd=JSDpS9UFAroXz0yoYgdKOezATjx20r.1 (https://riceuniversity.zoom.us/j/94882434656?pwd=JSDpS9UFAroXz0yoYgdKOezATjx20r.1)

item.title: "Còn nước, còn tát": The Ebbs and Flows of Water across Vietnamese Anglophone Cultural Production
item.description:
Thursday, July 2, 2026 at 6 PM

Location: Herring Hall

  Speaker: Karen Siu Doctoral Candidate Thesis Defense Department: English Location: Herring Hall 255 As a child and grandchild of Vietnamese refugees, my scholarship is personal and political, purposely following feminist and Ethnic Studies methodologies. It intertwines oral histories, creative nonfiction, and art with academic writing. This project's hybrid form is meant to be an innovation and contribution to the field of Vietnamese American Studies, which has often separated the genres of creative and academic writing rather than intertwining them. I ask, why water remains such an enduring symbol for Vietnamese diasporic writers, scholars, and artists, when it is often a painful reminder of war. When the capital of South Việt Nam, Sài Gòn, fell to the North Vietnamese Army, effectively ending the Việt Nam War on April 30, 1975, the date became known as ngày mất nước, the day we lost the country, we lost the water. In Vietnamese, nước (pronounced nyúk or \nü-äk\) means water, homeland, country, or nation. Fifty years after the war, it is clear that war and colonialism devastated the environment of Việt Nam, but what does it mean when the identity of the land, the water, and its people is also devastated? What does it mean to lose the water? This project pursues these questions at this poignant time. I examine the beginnings of nước in Vietnamese folklore alongside Vietnamese Anglophone Cultural Production, including literature, art, and war memorials and memorializations, in order to trace the transformation of nước before and after the Việt Nam War. I claim that Vietnamese Anglophone cultural works share an intimacy with water, deviating from a nước associated with the Vietnam War and towards a nước that instead emphasizes how our community survives and resists. I put forward nước as a cultural framework that is feminist, antiwar, and decolonial. Ultimately, I argue that these archives offer an ecofeminist, transcorporeal vision of hope and resilience in the wake of war, imperialism, and colonialism that gestures back to and expands on precolonial conceptions of nước. By ecofeminist and transcorporeal, I aim to show the fluidity and interconnectedness between gendered, reproductive bodies and water challenges the tendency to dichotomize humans and nature.   https://riceuniversity.zoom.us/j/99222333231?pwd=4PxBl3d0j2kfAz4vioBaDGTqZ23w1p.1 (Department : English)

Houston TCG Con IX — Your Favorite TCG Weekend in Texas Three days. 100+ vendors. Every game that matters. Houston TCG Con IX is back — and the Texas TCG community keeps showing up stronger every round! Pokémon. Yu-Gi-Oh!. Magic: The Gathering. One Piece. Lorcana. Riftbound. Dragon Ball Super. Digimon. Flesh and Blood. If it’s cardboard worth chasing, it’s on this floor.   📅 Date: Friday–Sunday, July 3–5, 2026 ⏰ Time: Friday: 3PM – 10PM | Saturday: 10AM – 6PM | Sunday: 10AM – 5PM 📍 Location: 9700 Bissonnet St, Unit 1500, Houston, TX 77036 🎟️ FREE Entry + FREE Parking   🔥 Event Highlights 🔥 🃏 100+ TCG Vendors — Three Days of Cardboard Shop from 100+ vendors traveling from across Texas and beyond. Find singles, sealed product, booster boxes, graded slabs, Japanese imports, and rare inserts across every major title — Pokémon, MTG, Yu-Gi-Oh!, One Piece, Lorcana, Riftbound, Dragon Ball Super, Digimon, Flesh and Blood, and more. Come with a wantlist. Leave with something you weren’t expecting.   🤝 The Trade Floor Is Open Bring your binders, your bulk, your trade bait, and your spec pulls. Houston TCG Con is a trade-friendly floor — deals happen naturally here because the people at the tables are collectors first. Three days means three days of opportunities to find the trade that makes your collection.   💸 Deals Across Every Budget From budget bulk and bargain boxes to high-end slabs and competitive singles — the floor has range. item.title: Novel Computational Representations of TCRs and pMHCs Incorporating Structural Information for Translational Applications
item.description:
Friday, July 3, 2026 at 2 PM

Location: Duncan Hall

  Speaker: Jared Slone Doctoral Candidate Thesis Defense Department: Computer Science Location: Duncan Hall 1049 The ability to accurately predict TCR-pHLA binding specificity would have major impacts on infectious disease research, autoimmunity, and personalized cancer immunotherapy. Experimental characterization of TCR-pHLA binding specificity is labor-intensive and costly, however, and, despite significant advances in machine learning, computationally predicting TCR binding specificity remains difficult. This difficulty arises from both the immense combinatorial space of possible TCR-pHLA pairings and the fact that the proteins in question possess high degrees of freedom, making it difficult to model their physical interactions. This thesis advances the computational characterization of TCR-pHLA interactions through the development of novel computational methods for modeling, representing, and predicting the behavior of TCR-pHLA pairs.   Central to this thesis is the use of three-dimensional structural information when predicting TCR-pHLA binding specificity. First, this thesis introduces STAG, a graph convolutional neural network for predicting TCR-pHLA binding specificity from modeled protein structures. STAG incorporates geometric and physicochemical information into a novel graph representation of TCR-pHLA complexes. The results from this work demonstrate that using holistic structural representations of the proteins as input outperforms prior structure-based approaches for binding prediction which were based on interprotein contacts. This thesis next presents STAG-LLM, a multimodal machine learning architecture that integrates protein language models with structure-based geometric learning. By combining amino acid sequence-derived embeddings with graph representations of three-dimensional protein structure, STAG-LLM achieves improved predictive performance and increased data efficiency compared to approaches that use sequence or structure alone. Recognizing that TCR-pHLA interactions are inherently dynamic, this thesis further introduces STEGG, a computational framework for generating structural ensembles of TCR-pHLA complexes. Through domain-specific conformational sampling and optimization techniques, STEGG efficiently explores the conformational landscape of TCR-pHLA interactions and generates diverse conformational ensembles without the computational expense of molecular dynamics simulations. Collectively, these contributions establish new computational paradigms for computationally representing TCR-pMHC structures, modeling their flexibility, and predicting their interactions. More broadly, this thesis demonstrates advances in structural bioinformatics that improve the computational characterization of TCR-pHLA interactions and aid in the development of personalized immunotherapies. (Department : Computer Science)

From Rice University - Open to the Public

item.title: Real and reciprocal space topology in rare earth intermetallics
item.description:
Friday, July 3, 2026 at 3 PM

Location: Brockman Hall for Physics

  Speaker: Kevin Allen Doctoral Candidate Thesis Defense Department: Physics & Astronomy Location: Brockman Hall for Physics 200 Topology in magnetic materials manifests across both real and reciprocal space, encompassing exotic band crossings in momentum space and nontrivial spin textures in real space. In strongly correlated and rare earth systems, these two aspects of topology are intertwined through the interplay of magnetic order, spin-orbit coupling, and electronic structure, giving rise to emergent phenomena including large anomalous Hall responses, extremely large magnetoresistance, and field-tunable soliton excitations. This dissertation presents four projects unified by this theme, spanning magnetic topological semimetals, atomically-sharp spin textures, strongly correlated heavy fermions, and spin-polarized surface catalysis. The first project identifies EuGa4 as a magnetic Weyl nodal ring semimetal, in which mirror symmetry protection stabilizes closed nodal line rings near the Fermi level in the spin-polarized state. Using angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES), quantum oscillation (QO) measurements, and density functional theory (DFT), we characterize the Weyl nodal ring states and their Landau quantization. The associated high carrier mobility gives rise to a transverse magnetoresistance ex- ceeding 200, 000% at 2 K and 14 T, more than two orders of magnitude large than any previously known magnetic topological semimetal, with non-saturating behavior up to 40 T explained by a theoretical model rooted in the nodal ring electronic structure. The second project demonstrates that the square-net rare earth compound EuRhAl4Si2 hosts atomically-sharp, field-tunable one-dimensional magnetic solitons stabilized by the competition between frustrated Ruderman-Kittel-Kasuya-Yosida (RKKY) exchange and uniaxial magnetic anisotropy. Magnetization measurements reveal a robust 1/3 magnetization plateau with fine steps corresponding to discrete soliton insertions, confirmed by anisotropic magnetotransport on a Focus ion beam (FIB)-fabricated microstructure. Neutron diffraction establishes the collinear ferrimagnetic ↑↑↓ ground state, while magnetic force microscopy (MFM) directly images the real space soliton lattice. First-principles exchange parameters and an effective J1–J2–K Heisenberg model, supplemented by Monte Carlo simulations, reproduce the observed field evolution and identify the soliton defects as the fundamental excitation. We further explore related square-net compounds by studying the series RTM4M'2 (R = Eu, Gd; T = Rh, Ir; M = Al, Ga; M' = Si, Ge), to identify additional candidates for hosting magnetic solitons and examine how structural and chemical tuning controls their stability. The third project reports a large anomalous Hall conductivity of approximately 1.6×104 Ω−1cm−1 in the centrosymmetric Kondo lattice compound Ce3Al11, a moderate heavy fermion with Kondo temperature TK = 10 K and a Sommerfeld coefficient of γ ≈ 100 mJ/molK2. The anomalous Hall effect (AHE) is stricly confined to the incommensurate antiferromagnetic phase below TN , and its temperature dependence reveals a crossover from extrinsic skew scattering near TN to an intrinsic Berry curvature dominated response at low temperature, consistent with the progressive onset of Kondo coherence. We attribute the symmetry breaking necessary to generate finite Berry curvature to the combination of incommensurate magnetic order and a Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interaction at the noncentrosymmetric CeII site establishing a route to large anomalous Hall responses in heavy fermion antiferromagnets without structural chirality or band inversion. The fourth project investigates how ferromagnetism in L10-CoPt enhances oxygen surface binding relevant to the oxygen reduction reaction. DFT calculations reveal than spin-polarized Co-d states, through exchange splitting of the minority spin channel toward the Fermi level, strengthen hybridization with O-p states and enhance adsorption energies by approximately 0.1 eV per oxygen atom relative to the non- magnetic case. By systematically varying the Pt overlayer thickness, we demonstrate that Co proximity to the surface amplifies these magnetic exchange effects, while the antiparallel oxygen spin configuration consistently yields the strongest binding. These results refine the d-band center model to incorporate spin-dependent hybridization and suggest pathways for optimizing catalytic performance through control of magnetic and structural degrees of freedom.https://riceuniversity.zoom.us/j/8841232928?pwd=Rys1Vm1TQ0RSR2twT1RiQkNzRDN2QT09 (https://riceuniversity.zoom.us/j/8841232928?pwd=Rys1Vm1TQ0RSR2twT1RiQkNzRDN2QT09) (Department : Physics & Astronomy)

From Rice University - Open to the Public

item.title: Frame of Reference in UAV Search and Rescue: Exocentric vs. Egocentric View for Navigation, Target Detection, and Collision Awareness
item.description:
Wednesday, July 8, 2026 at 4 PM

  Speaker: Yining Zhang Masters Candidate Thesis Defense Department: Psychology Unmanned Aerial Systems (UASs) have enabled search-and-rescue (SAR) missions to be safer, more efficient, and more effective due to their advanced mobility and sensing technologies. Traditionally, UASs employ a first-person, egocentric frame of reference (FOR), enabled by the camera system installed on the vehicle. However, this FOR provides limited visibility of the vehicle's surrounding environment, leading to blind spots. In contrast, research has shown that an exocentric, behind-the-vehicle FOR can lead to better mission outcomes and safer teleoperation. The current study compared operator performance and eye-movement patterns using the egocentric and exocentric FORs in a simulated SAR mission. A total of 37 participants performed three tasks using the two FORs, including collision-awareness, route-deviation detection, and target-detection tasks. The results showed that the effectiveness of the FORs was dependent on the task specifications. For the collision-awareness and target-detection tasks, there was no significant difference in response time (RT) or hit rate (H). For the route-deviation detection task, participants exhibited a speed-accuracy trade-off: they had a faster RT but lower H in the exocentric condition compared to the egocentric condition. Additionally, we found that participants in the exocentric condition attributed more visual attention to the camera view than in the egocentric condition, potentially due to the high salience of the vehicle body against the surrounding environment. Overall, the current study suggested that future UASs should employ a dynamic visual display, allowing the operator to choose the FOR that is more appropriate for the mission. https://riceuniversity.zoom.us/j/94483800467?pwd=XgoaQZA7ioB8HHGavYPyGnaDag6iDl.1 (Department : Psychology)

item.title: K-Nexus: Hierarchical Gene-to-Cell AI Modeling for Perturbation-Induced Transcriptomic Shift Recovery
item.description:
Wednesday, July 8, 2026 at 5 PM

  Speaker: Zihan Zhao Masters Candidate Thesis Defense Department: Electrical and Computer Engineering Predicting cellular responses to genetic and chemical perturbations can accelerate therapeutic discovery by reducing experimental screening. However, predicting post-perturbation gene expression, particularly for unseen perturbations, remains challenging because response signals are sparse, cellular responses are heterogeneous and often unpaired, and responsive genes are obscured by dominant non-responsive genes. We propose K-Nexus, a hierarchical gene-to-cell framework that formulates perturbation response prediction as distribution-shift recovery over responsive genes. K-Nexus encodes perturbation-conditioned regulatory interactions at the gene level and aggregates them into cell-level representations that preserve response heterogeneity. Attention-based gene importance, response-aware weighting, and group-based distributional training focus learning on genes driving perturbation-induced changes. K-Nexus also incorporates perturbation and regulatory graphs to capture functional relationships between targeted genes and downstream pathways. Across three single-cell perturbation benchmarks, K-Nexus outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, particularly in responsive-gene distributional alignment, demonstrating improved recovery of expression shifts and generalization to unseen perturbations.   (Department : Electrical and Computer Engineering)

From Rice University - Open to the Public

Event details

item.title: O⁺ Transport from the Ionospheric Cusp to the Inner Magnetosphere: A Global MHD and Test Particle Study
item.description:
Wednesday, July 8, 2026 at 5 PM

Location: Herman Brown Hall

  Speaker: Yiwen Zhu Doctoral Candidate Thesis Defense Department: Physics & Astronomy Location: Herman Brown Hall HBH 254 During geomagnetic storms, ionospheric oxygen (O⁺) can become a significant constituent of Earth's ring current, the current system whose buildup drives the main-phase depression of the surface magnetic field. The polar cusp is a principal source, feeding O⁺ outflow into the magnetotail lobe, but how much of this oxygen is delivered inward to the inner magnetosphere, and how it is energized along the way, remain poorly quantified, in part because the transport spans scales that no single model resolves.   In this thesis, we follow ionospheric oxygen from the cusp to the ring current using a global magnetohydrodynamic simulation (GAMERA/MAGE) coupled one-way to a test-particle tracer (CHIMP). Because MAGE includes an inner-magnetosphere model, it produces and resolves the mesoscale bursty bulk flows (BBFs) of the plasma sheet that drive earthward transport. With it, we identify a "drop-down" channel that controls the delivery to the inner magnetosphere: the dipolarization field carried by these flows pulls cusp oxygen from the lobe down to the equatorial plasma sheet, and the ions that experience it form a more efficient ring-current injection channel than those that convect down the tail unaided. Within an adiabatic (guiding-center) framework, we decompose the energy gain along the transport into its Fermi, betatron, and E×B contributions and quantify each, and we characterize where the oxygen departs from adiabatic behavior. To put these results on a quantitative footing, we weight the test particles by an O⁺ outflow rate computed directly from the simulation's MHD variables. For a real storm, this lets us quantify the absolute amount of cusp oxygen delivered to the inner magnetosphere and follow its contribution to the ring current through the storm, showing that a portion of the cusp oxygen is accelerated to ring-current energies and adds to the ring-current population. As a separate diagnostic, we characterize the energy and pitch-angle phase-space distributions, f(K, α), of the cusp oxygen at several locations along the transport and at ring-current entry, mapping how the population is energized and reshaped as it moves inward. Finally, we step back to the cusp itself. Forecasting where the cusp lies from the upstream solar wind is operationally important, yet no data-driven model has provided the cusp position directly. We develop a machine-learning model, gradient-boosted decision trees trained on DMSP spacecraft cusp crossings, that predicts the cusp location from solar-wind and interplanetary-magnetic-field conditions, offering a fast alternative to a full simulation. Together, these results advance two distinct fronts. The simulation and test-particle study closes a missing link in the cusp-to-ring-current pathway: it resolves how cusp oxygen reaches the inner magnetosphere, decomposes how the ions are energized along the way, and quantifies their absolute contribution to the storm-time ring current. The cusp machine-learning model is a separate, data-driven product: a fast predictor of the cusp location from the upstream solar wind, suited to operational use where a full simulation is impractical. Both improve how the entry of ionospheric heavy ions into the inner magnetosphere can be modeled and anticipated. (Department : Physics & Astronomy)

From Rice University - Open to the Public

item.title: Chemical Pathways to Earth's Habitability: Constraints from the Origin, Distribution, and Delivery of Life-Essential Elements in the Early Solar System
item.description:
Friday, July 10, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Keith-Wiess Geological Laboratories

  Speaker: Debjeet Pathak Doctoral Candidate Thesis Defense Department: Earth, Environmental and Planetary Sciences Location: Keith-Wiess Geological Laboratories The emergence and long-term habitability of Earth depend on the acquisition and redistribution of life-essential elements (LEEs), including C, N, P, S, H, and O, during planetary formation. This thesis investigates the chemical pathways that governed the origin, evolution, and delivery of these elements from the earliest stages of Solar System history to the formation of Earth. By integrating high-pressure-temperature experiments, meteoritic observations, geochemical reconstructions, and planetary accretion models, this work constrains the behavior of LEEs during core formation, planetary differentiation, and planetary growth. New experimental constraints on the partitioning of nitrogen and phosphorus between solid and liquid metallic alloys (Chapter 2 and 3), together with nitrogen partitioning between cohenite and metallic liquid (Chapter 4), are used to reconstruct the inventories of life-essential elements in the parent bodies of iron meteorites—the earliest differentiated planetesimals in the Solar System. Comparison of these reconstructed inventories with those of chondrites reveals the spatial and temporal evolution of life-essential elements during the earliest stages of Solar System formation and suggests that inner Solar system planetesimals were the major contributors of phosphorus and nitrogen to the growing Earth. Complementary electron probe microanalysis (EPMA) measurements significantly expand the existing nitrogen dataset for iron meteorites, providing improved constraints on their parent-body nitrogen inventories (Chapter 5). Furthermore, reconstructed zinc, sulfur, and gallium inventories provide evidence that disequilibrium condensation played an important role during the formation of these early planetary bodies (Chapter 6). Finally, numerical models of a Mars-sized last giant impactor demonstrate that its metallic core, rather than its silicate mantle, could have been an important source of volatile and life-essential elements delivered to the silicate Earth (Chapter 7). Collectively, this thesis provides new geochemical constraints on the origin, redistribution, and delivery of life-essential elements during planetary formation, offering a comprehensive framework for understanding the chemical pathways that ultimately led to Earth's habitability.   (Department : Earth, Environmental and Planetary Sciences)

item.title: Programmable control of small non-coding RNA expression in mammalian cells with synthetic RNA polymerase III transactivators.
item.description:
Friday, July 10, 2026 at 8 PM

Location: Keck Hall

  Speaker: Matthew Demelo Doctoral Candidate Thesis Defense Department: BioSciences-Biochemistry and Cell Biology Location: Keck Hall 102 Reliable control of gene expression via synthetic genetic circuits is essential to develop next-generation therapies, to facilitate efficient bioindustrial processes, and to enable advanced methods in basic cell biology. Synthetic control of small non-coding RNAs (sncRNAs) is of immense interest due to their important roles in development and disease in mammals, as well as their central role in technologies such as CRISPR/Cas gene editing and RNA interference. RNA polymerase III (Pol III) is the canonical polymerase for sncRNA expression, but few tools for regulated Pol III expression exist, requiring expression from RNA polymerase II (Pol II) promoters. While there has been extensive development of synthetic regulatory methods for expressing protein-coding RNAs and large non-coding RNAs via Pol II, these methods are often ineffective for robust expression of functional sncRNAs and often require post-transcriptional processing for sncRNAs to achieve functionality, impairing the feasibility of translating sncRNA technologies into medical and industrial applications. Here, I develop a toolkit capable of highly programmable and multiplexable expression of synthetic sncRNAs using Synthetic Pol III Transcription Factors (Syn3TFs), facilitating far more effective and robust expression of sncRNAs in mammals than previous methods. Additionally, I outline concise design rules for the construction of minimal Pol III promoters and complementary Syn3TFs, facilitating seamless construction of orthogonal Pol III circuits with customizable outputs. Finally, I demonstrate the utility of Syn3TFs in performing complex genetic logic and acting as efficient sensors for continuous molecular recording of cellular events. This platform represents a crucial first step to expanding the arsenal of circuit construction strategies for sncRNA expression and provides a strong foundation for continued development of Pol III-specific synthetic regulators.   (Department : BioSciences-Biochemistry and Cell Biology)

From Rice University - Open to the Public

item.title: Programmable control of small non-coding RNA expression in mammalian cells with synthetic RNA polymerase III transactivators.
item.description:
Friday, July 10, 2026 at 8 PM

Location: Keck Hall

  Speaker: Matt Demelo Doctoral Candidate Thesis Defense Department: BioSciences-Biochemistry and Cell Biology Location: Keck Hall 102 Reliable control of gene expression via synthetic genetic circuits is essential to develop next-generation therapies, to facilitate efficient bioindustrial processes, and to enable advanced methods in basic cell biology. Synthetic control of small non-coding RNAs (sncRNAs) is of immense interest due to their important roles in development and disease in mammals, as well as their central role in technologies such as CRISPR/Cas gene editing and RNA interference. RNA polymerase III (RNAP3) is the canonical polymerase for sncRNA expression, but few tools for regulated RNAP3 expression exist, so genetic circuits requiring regulated sncRNA expression must use RNA polymerase II (RNAP2) promoters. While there has been extensive development of synthetic regulatory methods for expressing protein-coding RNAs and large non-coding RNAs via RNAP2, these methods are often ineffective for robust expression of functional sncRNAs and often require post-transcriptional processing for sncRNAs to achieve functionality, impairing the feasibility of translating sncRNA technologies into medical and industrial applications. Here, we develop a toolkit capable of highly programmable and multiplexable expression of synthetic sncRNAs using Synthetic RNAP3 Transcription Factors (Syn3TFs), achieving greater than 10-fold improvement in efficiency compared to analogous RNAP2-mediated methods. Additionally, we outline concise design rules for the construction of minimal RNAP3 promoters and complementary Syn3TFs, facilitating seamless construction of orthogonal RNAP3 circuits with customizable outputs. Finally, we demonstrate the utility of Syn3TFs in performing complex genetic logic and acting as efficient sensors for continuous molecular recording of cellular events. This platform represents a crucial first step to expanding the arsenal of circuit construction strategies for sncRNA expression and provides a strong foundation for continued development of RNAP3-specific synthetic regulators.   (Department : BioSciences-Biochemistry and Cell Biology)

From Rice University - Open to the Public

item.title: Probabilistic Analysis of Nonstationary Hydroclimate Extremes
item.description:
Wednesday, July 15, 2026 at 3 PM

Location: Ryon Engineering Building

  Speaker: Yuchen Lu Doctoral Candidate Thesis Defense Department: Civil and Environmental Engineering Location: Ryon Engineering Building 112 Hydroclimate extremes, including extreme rainfall and tropical cyclones, pose substantial threats to communities, infrastructure systems, and ecosystems. Probabilistic characterization of these extremes is essential for risk assessment, infrastructure design, mitigation planning, and climate adaptation. Many commonly used approaches for estimating hydroclimate hazards assume stationarity, even though observations and climate projections show that extreme events are influenced by climate variability and long-term climate change. However, accounting for this nonstationarity poses methodological challenges: observations of rare events are sparse in time and space, while climate model products contain systematic biases. To address these challenges, this dissertation develops statistical frameworks that incorporate nonstationarity into probabilistic analyses of hydroclimate extremes using limited observational records.   The first chapter develops a hierarchical Bayesian space-time framework for nonstationary analysis of extreme rainfall probabilities. This framework mitigates the challenge of larger sampling variability and uncertainty by incorporating time-varying climate covariates and spatial pooling, allowing information to be shared across stations while estimating spatially coherent changes in daily extreme rainfall. The second chapter extends this analysis to multi-duration rainfall frequency estimates by incorporating duration dependence. This work addresses the challenge of maintaining coherence across both space and durations, which is especially important for short-duration rainfall extremes where observations are particularly sparse. The third chapter develops a nonstationary joint probability framework for characterizing tropical cyclone parameters relevant to hazard analysis. This approach mitigates the challenge of limited local tropical cyclone observations by using regional changes in storm characteristics to inform local-scale analyses and by linking dependent storm parameters through physically motivated relationships. Together, these three studies address a common problem in hydroclimate risk analysis: how to estimate changing extremes from limited and spatially heterogeneous observations. Across rainfall and tropical cyclone applications, the proposed frameworks improve the stability, spatial coherence, and physical interpretability of nonstationary hazard estimates. These methods provide a basis for more robust infrastructure design and climate adaptation planning under changing hydroclimate risk. (Department : Civil and Environmental Engineering)

From Rice University - Open to the Public

item.title: Cobalt Photocatalysis as a Platform for Tunable Hydrogen Atom Transfer in the Ring Opening of Cyclic Ethers
item.description:
Friday, July 17, 2026 at 8 PM

Location: BioScience Research Collaborative

  Speaker: Brian Funk Doctoral Candidate Thesis Defense Department: Chemistry Location: BioScience Research Collaborative 1060 A/B This dissertation explores thoughtful design and development of synthetic methods photocatalyzed by Vitamin B12 and analogous cobaloximes. Central to these protocols is control over diverging HAT from Earth-abundant Cobalt complexes and simple organic thiols, shown to be heavily influenced by proticity of the reaction environment. Through strategic control of HAT directionality, these works provide mild, complementary reactive scaffolds to classical hydrogenation by hydride nucleophiles and carbonyl production by traditional Meinwald rearrangements. The addition of these new techniques to the synthetic chemist's toolbox has the potential to improve the limited accessibility of hydride-like reactivity in chemical synthesis, influence the perception of cyclic ethers as precursors to valuable functional handles, and further advance the onset of VB12 and its derivatives as powerful photocatalysts. Armed with the strong mechanistic foundation elucidated throughout this dissertation, further application of Cobalt photocatalysis in broader substrate classes can maximize the impact of this powerful, sustainable, and tunable reactivity.   (Department : Chemistry)

From Rice University - Open to the Public

item.title: CCS Nerd Yard Sale III
item.description:
Saturday, July 18, 2026 at 10 AM

CCS Houston Nerd Yard Sale III — Dig In. Deal Out. Pass It On. Got shelves full of stuff that deserves a new home? Looking to score fan-loved finds at yard-sale prices? CCS Houston Nerd Yard Sale III is back for a full weekend — two days of clearing, digging, dealing, and connecting with fellow fans across every fandom and era. This isn’t a convention. It’s not a vendor hall. It’s a true community yard sale where real collectors clear their collections, real deals get made, and real conversations happen over shared fandoms.   📅 Date: Saturday–Sunday, July 18–19, 2026 ⏰ Time: Saturday: 10AM – 6PM | Sunday: 10AM – 5PM 📍 Location: 9700 Bissonnet St, Unit 1500, Houston, TX 77036 🎟️ FREE Entry + FREE Parking   🔥 Event Highlights 🔥 🧺 For Sellers — Clear Your Shelves, Make Some Cash Got boxes of fandom stuff ready to move? Duplicates, impulse buys, collections you’ve outgrown? Set up a table, meet fellow fans, and pass your collection on to someone who’ll love it just as much as you did. Perfect for collectors downsizing, families clearing closets, and anyone who needs to make space. 👉 Apply to be a seller   🔍 For Shoppers — Dig, Deal, Discover Bargain hunters, this floor is yours. Browse tables packed with fan-loved finds at yard-sale prices across every fandom and era. You never know what you’ll find — but here’s what might be waiting: 📚 Comics, manga, and books 🎮 Retro games and consoles — NES, SNES, Sega, PlayStation classics, and more 🧸 Plushies, pins, keychains, and gacha 🧩 Anime merch, figures, and Funkos 📼 VHS, vintage media, and old-school collectibles 👕 Fandom fashion and accessories 🃏 Cards, toys, and unexpected gems Prices are yard-sale friendly. Every table is different. The best finds are the ones you didn’t know you were looking for.   👨‍👩‍👧‍👦 All Fandoms. All Ages. Zero Gatekeeping. Bring the whole crew. No pressure. No gatekeeping. Just fans connecting with fans over the things they love.   🏨 Traveling to Houston? Lock In Your Rate We’ve secured a special hotel rate exclusively for Nerd Yard Sale III attendees. Book your stay and make it a full weekend. 👉 Book here   Bring your trade boxes. Bring your shopping bags. Bring your inner treasure hunter. CCS Houston Nerd Yard Sale III — July 18 & 19.

item.title: Houston World of Hot Wheels IV
item.description:
Saturday, July 25, 2026 at 10 AM

🚗🏁 Houston World of Hot Wheels IV is roaring back with more thrills and speed! 🏁🚗 After an electric, action-packed weekend of nonstop deals, racing, and collector excitement, Houston World of Hot Wheels IV is revving up for another round! We’re bringing even MORE vendors, MORE cars, and MORE action for collectors, racers, and families to enjoy all weekend long. ⚡   📅 July 25–26, 2026 | 10AM–5PM 📍 9700 Bissonnet St 1500, Houston, TX 🎟️ FREE Entry + FREE Parking   Event Highlights 🛒 Hot Wheels Swap & Shop – Both Days 50+ vendors. 30,000+ Hot Wheels, Matchbox, and diecast to dig through. STHs, TH variations, premium Car Culture, redline-era pieces, MOC vintage, loose mainline bins, M2s, Greenlights, Johnny Lightnings. Bring your want list!   🚗✨ Houston Diecast Custom Contest – 10AM-3PM | Both Days Calling all builders and custom kings 👑 Bring your best custom Hot Wheels/Matchbox build — paint, wheel swaps, stance, body kits, full one-of-one creations. This contest is for the detail freaks and the true “trust the process” customizers. 🏆 The winner will be announced at 4PM and will receive a prize sponsored by Domingo! ✨ Hosted by @funopolistoys   🏁 Hot Wheels Racing Contest – Both Days The stage track is live all weekend. Hourly races, prizes for winners, FREE to enter.   🏎️ Car Show (Parking Lot Showcase) Take it outside and check out Houston’s coolest real-life rides. Style, horsepower, and car-community energy all in one spot.   👶 Mini Zones & Family Fun Fun spaces for younger collectors and first‑time racers — all ages welcome!   🚨 VENDORS WANTED Got STHs, vintage redlines, custom builds, or bulk mainline to move? This is your crowd. 📥 Apply: https://tinyurl.com/CCSGuestVendorFromHouston   🏨 Special Hotel Deals Enjoy a special room rate of $79/night at TownePlace Suites, just about 15 minutes from the venue! 👉 Book: https://www.marriott.com/event-reservations/reservation-link.mi?id=1767823967549&key=CORP&app=resvlink   Hunt or race. Trade or just walk the floor. The customs will be parked outside, the bins will be open inside, and the track will be running all weekend. See you July 25–26.

From CCS Houston

item.title: Art + Climate CONVERSATIONS: Emily Johnson
item.description:
Wednesday, July 29, 2026 at 6:30 PM

Location: DiverseWorks – MATCHBOX 2, 3400 Main Street, Houston, TX, 77002, United States

Presented by DW Art + Climate Partners 6:30 pm, Wednesday, July 29, 2026 Suggested Ticket Price: $25 (pay-what-you-wish) Get Tickets 10% of ticket sales will be donated to the Texas Tribal Buffalo Project The DiverseWorks Art + Climate Partners present Emily Johnson as the inaugural speaker for Art + Climate CONVERSATIONS. Join Houstonians, climate advocates, dance and art enthusiasts, and artists alike on the evening of July 29th for a talk by Johnson, a Yu’pik multi-disciplinary artist, choreographer, land and water protector, and organizer for justice, sovereignty and well-being. In October 2026, Johnson’s Overflow Radio will be presented at DiverseWorks. Art + Climate CONVERSATIONS highlights artists and creatives that are leading the way on addressing climate-related issues through their work. The DW Art + Climate Partners support and advise on climate programming and future projects. DiverseWorks’ Art + Climate program began in 2023-24 and consists of a climate action plan that guides the organization; programming that increases awareness of the effects of climate change; support for artists to develop environmentally sustainable practices; and the development of a new, climate-forward facility for artists, community members, and other non-profit organizations to share. Speaker Bio Emily Johnson is a Bessie Award-winning choreographer, Guggenheim, Native Arts and Cultures Foundation, and United States Artists Fellow, and recipient of the Doris Duke Artist Award. She is based in Lenapehoking / New York City. Emily is of the Yup’ik Nation, and since 1998 has created work that considers the experience of sensing and seeing performance. Her dances function as portals and care processions, they engage audienceship within and through space, time, and environment — interacting with a place’s architecture, peoples, history and role in building futures. Emily is trying to make a world where performance is part of life; where performance is an integral part of our connection to each other, our environment, our stories, our past, present and future. Her choreography and gatherings have been presented across what is currently called the United States and Australia. Her large-scale project, Then a Cunning Voice and A Night We Spend Gazing at Stars is an all-night outdoor performance gathering taking place amongst 84 community-hand-made quilts. It premiered in Lenapehoking (NYC) in 2017, and was presented in Zhigaagoong (Chicago) in 2019. She choreographed the Santa Fe Opera production of Doctor Atomic, directed by Peter Sellars in 2018. Being Future Being, premiered on Tongva Land in Los Angeles in 2022. In 2011, DiverseWorks presented her performance The Thank-You Bar in conjunction with the exhibition This is Displacement: Native Artists Consider the Relationship Between Land and Identity (co-curated by Johnson and Diné visual artist Carolyn Lee Anderson).  Emily’s writing has been published and commissioned by The Open Society University Network’s Center for Human Rights and the Arts, ArtsLink Australia, unMagazine, Dance Research Journal (University of Cambridge Press); SFMOMA; Transmotion Journal, University of Kent; Movement Research Journal; Pew Center for Arts and Heritage; and the compilation Imagined Theaters: Writing for a Theoretical Stage (Routledge). Emily hosts monthly ceremonial fires on Mannahatta in partnership with Abrons Arts Center and Kai Recollet. She was the Pueblo Opera Cultural Council Diplomat at Santa Fe Opera 2018-2020, and a lead organizer of First Nations Dialogues. She was a co-compiler of the documents, Creating New Futures: Guidelines for Ethics and Equity in the Performing Arts and Notes for Equitable Funding, was a member of Creative Time’s inaugural Think Tank, and serves as a working consortium member for First Nations Performing Arts.

From DiverseWorks

item.title: Rice University Summer Engineering Innovation Program - Demo Day 2026
item.description:
Thursday, July 30, 2026 at 11 PM

Location: The Ion Houston

About the Summer Engineering Innovation Program at Rice UniversityRice University's Summer Engineering Innovation Program (http://www.rcelconnect.org/seip) is a dynamic, hands-on experience for students to solve real-world challenges using Artificial Intelligence, Digital Engineering, Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE), and Industry 4.0 technologies. Over 10 weeks, students will collaborate with industry leaders, sharpen technical skills, and bring breakthrough ideas to life through project-based learning and mentorship. Whether developing cutting-edge prototypes or designing smart systems, this program empowers students to turn theory into impact.

item.title: CCS $20,000 cEDH Invitational Qualifier #3
item.description:
Saturday, August 1, 2026 at 9 AM

🏆 CCS $20,000 cEDH Invitational Qualifier #3 🏆 The race tightens. The margins shrink. Qualifier #3 brings us into the mid season of the CCS $50,000 cEDH Invitational Series. With only three events after, every decision matters and every point is gold. Full Details → https://topdeck.gg/event/ccs-50000-cedh-invitational   🗓 Dates: August 1–2, 2026 📍 Location: Charlie’s Collectible Show, 9700 Bissonett St Unit 1500, Houston, TX 77036 Tournament Details: 🎟️ Entry Fee: $90 before July 18, $110 After 🔗 Register Now! 👉  https://topdeck.gg/event/css-20000-cedh-invitational-qualifier-4 🗂️ Format & Structure Format: cEDH (Proxy Friendly) Saturday: 6 Swiss Rounds (based on attendance) Sunday: 2 Swiss Rounds → Top 16 🚫 Proxy Requirements: Full card name, mana cost, type line, and effect text Test cards or clearly written MDFCs allowed No paper slip-ins permitted 💰 Guaranteed $20,000 Prize Pool 1st — $5,000 + Auto Invite + TopDeck Invite + 200 pts 2nd-4th — $2,000 each + Auto Invite + 160 pts 5th–8th — $550 + 120 pts 9th–16th — $550 + 100 pts 17th–32nd — $175 + 75 pts 33rd–48th — 60 pts 49th–64th — 45 pts 65th–96th — 30 pts 97th–128th — 20 pts Event Schedule: 🕔 Registration Opens: 9 AM 🕔 Player Meeting (Day 1): 10 AM 🕔 Day 2 Start Time: 9 AM 🛍️ Collectible Marketplace Between rounds, enjoy access to a curated marketplace featuring: TCG singles, sealed, and accessories Retro video games & consoles Anime merch, figures, and models Toys and other hobby collectibles Perfect for grabbing upgrades, browsing grails, or just hanging out between matches. 🏨 Special Hotel Deals Enjoy a special rate of $79/night — 2 Queens at TownePlace Suites, only 15 minutes distance from the action! Book your stay 👉🏻 https://www.marriott.com/event-reservations/reservation-link.mi?id=1767823967549&key=CORP&app=resvlink 🔔 IMPORTANT REMINDERS Player names will appear as real names on published documents. Top 16 decklists will be published. 🔥 Late-season stakes hit hard — tighten your game and push forward. Your run to the $50K Invitational continues at CCS cEDH Qualifier #3!

From CCS Houston

Event details

item.title: Houston Comic Exchange II
item.description:
Saturday, August 1, 2026 at 10 AM

Houston Comic Exchange II – Built for Comic Collectors Summer is prime comic-hunting season, and CCS Houston Comic Exchange II brings together collectors, dealers, and fans for a day of buying, selling, trading, and discovering comics. From key issues and first appearances to hidden gems and box-fresh finds, every table offers something worth digging for. No gatekeeping, no pressure, just comics and the people who love them.   📅 Saturday, August 1, 2026 | 10AM – 5PM 📍 9700 Bissonnet St, Unit 1500, Houston, TX 🎟️ FREE Entry + FREE Parking   🔥 Event Highlights 🔥 📦 50 Vendor Tables – All Comics, All Formats Singles, back issues, key issues, first appearances, graded slabs, raw runs, indie and small press, variant covers. Vendor tables dedicated entirely to comics, no toys, no filler. If you’re a comic collector, this floor was built for you.   🔍 Key Issues and First Appearances This is where the hunt lives. Vendors bring the deep cuts, raw back issues worth digging through, graded books if you’re chasing condition, and the kind of keys that actually show up when serious collectors are clearing their own collections to make room.   📚 Back-Issue Digging and Indie Discovery Not every great find is a grail. Long boxes of back issues across decades and publishers, plus indie comics and small press titles that never make it to a standard shop shelf. Come with a list or come ready to browse – both work.   🤝 Vendors Who Collect Too The people behind these tables are the same people who will ask what you’re hunting before they start pulling books for you. The Houston Comic Exchange is a community floor first. The conversations are part of the event.   🛒 Vendor Table Applications Houston comic vendors: we’re gathering a focused comics-only crowd, and free entry is driving serious foot traffic. Apply for your table here 👉🏻 https://forms.gle/7q6pjcoiJTPbJouP8   The key issue you’ve been tracking is probably in one of those long boxes this summer. The Houston Comic Exchange II, August 1. Free entry, free parking, always.

From CCS Houston

item.title: Houston Toy Exchange III
item.description:
Sunday, August 2, 2026 at 5 AM

Houston Toy Exchange III — The Floor Is Loaded, Houston Somewhere on this floor is the piece you have been hunting for months. 🔥 Houston Toy Exchange III is a pure toy marketplace, no convention noise, no filler. Just collectors, families, and dealers moving action figures, vintage finds, and modern grails across one packed floor at CCS Houston. The fun is in the hunt: digging through a bin you have never seen, talking shop with someone who knows the line better than you do, and walking out with something you did not expect to find.   📅 Date: Sunday, August 2, 2026 | 10AM – 5PM 📍 Location: 9700 Bissonnet St, #1500, Houston, TX 77036 🎟️ FREE Entry + FREE Parking   🔥 What You’ll Find on the Floor 🔥 🛍️ Toy Marketplace Tables packed with action figures, vintage toys, sealed and loose, nostalgia pieces, and the modern lines collectors are actually chasing right now. Every table is its own dig. Come search the floor.   💰 Buy, Sell, Trade Bring the toys you are ready to pass along or trade up for something you have been chasing. Collectors and vendors are buying, selling, and trading all day. Connect, negotiate, and keep great pieces moving through the community.   🔍 Toy Appraisals Holding something and not sure what it’s worth? One of our experienced vendors is running toy appraisals all day, sharing insights on value, rarity, and trade potential so you can make confident, informed deals on the floor.   🧩 Toy Scavenger Hunt Challenge Turn the hunt into a game. Work the marketplace and complete the Toy Hunt Challenge by tracking down specific toy categories across multiple booths for a shot at prizes and collector bragging rights.   📸 Toy Flex Wall Score something special? Show it off. Snap a photo of your best pickup at the Toy Flex Wall and share your find with the community.   Longtime collector or just starting out, Houston Toy Exchange III is about connecting, swapping stories, and keeping the love of toys alive together. Bring your toys, bring your curiosity, pull up.   Want in as a vendor? Tables are open: https://tinyurl.com/CCSGuestVendorFromHouston   Houston Toy Exchange III. Bring your toys, bring your curiosity, and come move some pieces.

From CCS Houston

item.title: Houston TCG Con X
item.description:
Friday, August 7, 2026 at 10 AM

🃏 Houston TCG Con X | Every Format. One Floor. Three Days. 🃏 Three days. Every format in the room. The Houston TCG community takes over CCS August 7–9 for Pokémon, MTG, One Piece, Lorcana, and Yu-Gi-Oh. Tournament grinders and binder flippers on the same floor, doing what this community does best.   📅 Date: Friday–Sunday, August 7–9, 2026 ⏰ Time: Friday: 3PM–10PM | Saturday: 10AM–6PM | Sunday: 10AM–5PM 📍 Location: 9700 Bissonnet St, Unit 1500, Houston, TX 🎟️ FREE Entry + FREE Parking   🔥 Event Highlights 🔥 🃔 Multi-Game Vendor Floor Pokémon. MTG. One Piece. Lorcana. Yu-Gi-Oh. Singles vendors, sealed product, and binder traders across every major format. Three full days to hunt, flip, and deal face-to-face.   🏆 Multi-Game Tournaments Competitive rounds running all weekend across formats. Tournament schedule and registration details coming soon. Follow CCS Houston for updates.   📦 Full Marketplace Sports cards, anime merch, figures, comics, and toys rounding out the vendor floor for everything you collect beyond the card game.   💼 Vendor Tables Available TCG dealers, singles vendors, sports card sellers, anime and collectible booths. Houston’s biggest TCG weekend is yours. Apply: https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLSdei0apYj2qU4fQIiz9rrqVDHgqmFZSpLRzYDAufcg3B8BaXA/viewform   🏨 Traveling to Houston? Lock Your Room Group rate available at Marriott. Book before it fills: https://www.marriott.com/event-reservations/reservation-link.mi?id=1767823967549&key=CORP&app=resvlink Three days, every format, free entry. See you in August, Houston.

item.description:
Saturday, August 8, 2026 at 10 AM

🏆 Legacy Duelist Series Houston🏆 Charlie’s Collectible Show is proud to partner with Yggdrasil Materias to bring the Legacy Duelist Series to Houston! If you’re a duelist who loves retro formats, then this weekend is a must. LDS Houston features an Edison main event, an Edison 3v3 Sunday Event, and a Goat format Sunday tournament. Yggdrasil Materias will be slinging supplies, and CCS will be providing the Yu-Gi-Oh! Singles.   🗓 Dates: August 8-9, 2026 📍 Location: Charlie’s Collectible Show, 9700 Bissonett St Unit 1500, Houston, TX 77036   Tournament Details: 🎟️ Main Event Entry Fee: $60 (SOLD OUT) 🎟️ Day 2 Edison 3v3 Entry Fee: $115 🎟️ Day 2 Goat Entry Fee: $40 🔗 Main Event Page 👉  https://www.yggdrasilmaterias.com/product/lds-houston-ygo-tournament-day-1-edison-format 🔗 Register For Sunday Edison 3v3 Now! 👉  https://www.yggdrasilmaterias.com/product/lds-houston-ygo-tournament-day-2-3v3-edison 🔗 Register For Sunday Goat Format Now! 👉 https://www.yggdrasilmaterias.com/product/lds-houston-ygo-tournament-day-2-goat   🗂️ Format & Structure Formats: Day 1: Edison Main Event | Day 2: Main Event Top Cut, Edison 3v3 & Goat Format Day 1 — Edison Tournament: 9 rounds of Swiss Day 2 — Edison Tournament Top Cut Day 2 — Edison 3v3 Tournament 6 rounds of Swiss with cut to Top 8 Day 2 — Goat Tournament: 7 rounds of Swiss with cut to Top 8   💰 Edison Format Prizing: 1st: SJCS Dark End Dragon + First Place Playmat + LDS Champs Invite 2nd: Ultimate Rare Caius The Shadow Monarch x2 + Top 8 Playmat + LDS Champs Invite 3rd: Ultimate Rare Caius The Shadow Monarch + Top 8 Playmat + LDS Champs Invite 4th: Ultimate Rare Dark Armed Dragon x1 + Top 8 Playmat + LDS Champs Invite 5th – 8th: Ultimate Rare Judgment Dragon x1 + Top 8 Playmat + LDS Champs Invite 9th -16th: Colossal Fighter Ultimate Rare x1 + Top 16 Playmat 17th-32nd: Tragoedia Ultimate Rare x1 + Top 32 Playmat   💰 Edison 3v3 Prizing: 1st Place Team: Ultimate Rare Dark Armed Dragon x3 + Top Cut Playmat x3 2nd Place Team: Champion Pack Trap Dustshoot x3 + Top Cut Playmat x3 3rd-4th Place Team: TBD 5th-8th Place Team: TBD   💰 Goat Format Prizing: 1st: TP2 Morphing Jar + First Place Playmat 2nd: Champion Pack Book of Moon + Top Cut Playmat 3rd-4th: TP7 Breaker the Magical Warrior + Top Cut Playmat 5th – 8th: Ultimate Rare Tsukuyomi + Top Cut Playmat   Day 1 Event Schedule: 🕔 Doors Open: 7:00 AM 🕔 Check-In Cutoff: 8:30 AM 🕔 Start Time: 8:45 AM   Day 2 Event Schedule: 🕔 Doors Open: 7:00 AM 🕔 Main Event Top Cut Start: 7:30 AM 🕔 Goat Check-In Cutoff: 7:45 AM 🕔 Goat Tournament Start: 8:00 AM 🕔 Edison 3v3 Check-In Cutoff: 8:45 AM 🕔 Edison 3v3 Tournament Start 9:00 AM

item.title: Jesus Benavente: NUESTROWORLD
item.description:
Thursday, August 13, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: DiverseWorks, @ 5601 B Navigation Blvd & Various Locations, 5601 B Navigation Blvd, Houston, TX, 77011, United States

Jesus Benavente, A life, A Home and A Horse Named Triste, 2021, courtesy of the artist. Opening Thursday, August 13, 2026 | 6 – 8 PM Gallery Hours: Thurs – Sat 12 – 6 PM NUESTROWORLD is a satirical solo exhibition by Brooklyn-based artist Jesus Benavente, conceived of as a visual album and immersive “amusement park.” This project features interconnected works unfolding across the gallery and city, collectively highlighting Houston’s cyclical history of ambition, folly, and heartbreak. The title NUESTROWORLD is a play on Astroworld and uses Spanglish to create the phrase “The World Is Ours.” The project draws inspiration from popular events that mark specific eras of Houston’s history, such as the opening of the world-famous Astrodome and Astroworld Park in the late 60’s, Selena’s iconic Rodeo performances in the 90’s, and the folklore surrounding the 1836 capture of Santa Anna during the Battle of San Jacinto as part of the Texas Revolution. Together, they build a story, a world developed over time that underscores sacrifice, instills resilience, and highlights a Texas attitude of “come and take it.”  ARTIST BIO: Jesus Benavente is a visual artist based in Brooklyn, New York. Jesus earned an MFA from Rutgers University and attended Skowhegan School of Painting and Sculpture. Recent exhibitions and performances include the Whitney Museum of American Art, New York; Queens Museum, Queens; LTD Los Angeles, Los Angeles; Performa 13, New York; Acre Projects, Chicago; Chinati Foundation, Marfa; the Neuberger Museum of Art, Purchase, NY; Shin Museum of Art, South Korea; Vox Populi, Philadelphia; Socrates Sculpture Park, New York; and Austin Museum of Art. Benavente was born in San Antonio, Texas.  

From DiverseWorks

item.title: Retro Video Game Expo III
item.description:
Saturday, August 15, 2026 at 10 AM

🕹️ Houston, Press Start on a Weekend of Pure Retro Nostalgia! 👾 Step back into the golden era of gaming at Retro Video Game Expo III, a two-day celebration of classic consoles, arcade legends, competitive retro tournaments, and nonstop free play. From expanded free-play zones spanning the 80s and beyond to live, high-energy tournament matchups and a collector-focused marketplace, this expo is built for gamers who chase high scores and grail finds.   🗓️ Date & Time: August 15–16, 2026 | 10AM–5PM 📍 Location: 9700 Bissonnet St #1500, Houston, TX 77036 💥 FREE ENTRY. FREE PARKING. FREE PLAY. 🎮 Event Highlights 🎮 Retro Video Game Marketplace – All Weekend Step into a collector’s paradise. Vendors from across the region are bringing vintage consoles, original cartridges, imports, manuals, accessories, and hard-to-find titles. This is where treasures respawn. 🚨 Want to vend? Sign up here 👉 https://tinyurl.com/HoustonVendors   🎮 Free-Play Zone – ALL Day Jump on 80’s Arcades & Classic Consoles and relive the games that made gaming legendary. More setups and activities to be announced soon! 🤝 Calling All Collaborators & Retro Communities This event is built with the community for community! We’re actively looking to partner with: 🏁 Tournament organizers → https://forms.gle/74Qvuv9PFzeDVF1V8 🕹️ Pinball/Arcade Hosts → https://forms.gle/74Qvuv9PFzeDVF1V8 🎤 Workshops & Panels → https://forms.gle/q2nCKsZ6FEVZBgqW9 🙌 Volunteer Sign-Up → https://forms.gle/15zMGTqZPSGKjGdy6 🎮 Vendor Applications → https://tinyurl.com/CCSGuestVendorFromHouston If you’ve ever wanted to be part of event, bring your passion! Let’s chat! 📩   🏨 Special Hotel Deals Enjoy a special rate of $79/night — 2 Queens at TownePlace Suites, only 15 minutes distance from the action! Book your stay 👉🏻 https://www.marriott.com/event-reservations/reservation-link.mi?id=1767823967549&key=CORP&app=resvlink   Bring your crew, grab a controller, and let’s relive the golden age of gaming together. 👾✨

From CCS Houston

item.title: Triple Crown Celebration
item.description:
Thursday, August 27, 2026 at 9 PM

Location: Duncan Hall

Join us in honoring Lydia Kavraki (https://news.rice.edu/news/2025/national-academy-sciences-elects-lydia-kavraki-member) and Rebecca Richards-Kortum (https://news.rice.edu/news/2025/richards-kortum-elected-national-academy-medicine), two scholars whose careers exemplify the highest ideals of research, innovation, mentorship and service. Through groundbreaking discoveries, commitment to students, and work that has improved lives around the world, they have advanced their fields and inspired the next generation of researchers, engineers and scientists. They are also the only two researchers in Texas elected to all three National Academies, an extraordinary distinction held by fewer than 35 living researchers nationwide. The program will feature reflections from university leadership, perspectives from colleagues, and a conversation with the honorees on the work, values and experiences that have shaped their remarkable careers.RSVP for this special event! (https://engrice.wufoo.com/forms/the-triple-crown-of-discovery/)

From Rice University - Open to the Public

item.title: CCS Card Show I
item.description:
Saturday, August 29, 2026 at 10 AM

CCS Card Show I — Every Sport. Every TCG. One Floor. Houston, the hobby comes home. For the first time at CCS, every major sport and every major TCG land under one roof: sports card collectors, memorabilia hunters, and TCG players moving cards on the same floor, the way it should be. Modern hits to vintage grails, sealed wax to graded slabs, raw singles to game-used pieces. Buy, sell, trade, grade. This is the room.   📅 Date: August 29-30, 2026 | 10AM – 5PM 📍 Location: 9700 Bissonnet St, Unit 1500, Houston, TX 77036 🎟️ FREE Entry + FREE Parking   🔥 Event Highlights 🔥 ⚾ The Ultimate Sports Card & Memorabilia Marketplace Explore vendors dedicated to sports cards, high-end memorabilia, and rare collectibles from baseball, football, basketball, and more. If you’re hunting for a signed rookie card, vintage set, or exclusive autograph, this is THE place to level up your collection.   🃏 Trading Card Game Extravaganza 🃏 We’re also featuring top TCG vendors offering a treasure trove of Pokémon, Yu-Gi-Oh!, Magic: The Gathering, One Piece, Lorcana, and more! Chase that elusive rare pull or build your deck for your next tournament, you’ll find the best deals on booster packs, graded singles, sealed products, and Japanese imports.   💼 Vendors Wanted Got a table’s worth to move? Guest vendors are welcome at Card Show I. The person across the table is a fan like you. Claim your spot 👉🏻 https://forms.gle/sZdrRxHkEXM69kbN6   🏨 Traveling to Houston? Lock In Your Rate Enjoy a special rate of $79/night — ****2 Queens at TownePlace Suites, only 15 minutes away from the action! 👉 https://www.marriott.com/event-reservations/reservation-link.mi?id=1767823967549&key=CORP&app=resvlink   Houston’s first CCS Card Show. Every sport, every TCG, one floor. Pull up.

From CCS Houston

item.title: Rice360 Global Health Technologies Seminar Series: AI Driven Wearable Sensors for Maternal and Newborn Health in Low Resource Settings
item.description:
Tuesday, September 1, 2026 at 10 PM

Location: BioScience Research Collaborative

Join Rice360 for engaging conversations with leaders in global health and development as they share their expert insights on the intersection of medicine, technology and policy. Steve Xu, MD, MSc is a physician-engineer, entrepreneur, and board-certified dermatologist. He is the CEO and co-founder of Sibel Health as well as the Ruth K. Freinkel, MD Professor at Northwestern University. He received his undergraduate degree in bioengineering from Rice University summa cum laude, an MD from Harvard Medical School (Soros Fellow), and a Masters in Health Policy from The London School of Economics (Marshall Scholar). Dr. Xu has authored more than 120 peer-reviewed publications and listed as an inventor on 15 pending and granted patents. As both an NIH, Gates Foundation, FDA, and DoD funded investigator, he has led complex engineering, artificial intelligence, and clinical development efforts worldwide. As an MIT 35 Under 35 Technology innovator, his publications have appeared in Nature, Science, The Proceedings of the National Academy of Medicine, and the New England Journal of Medicine garnering press attention from The New York Times, CNN, The Washington Post, and The Los Angeles Times. Within 4 years of founding, Sibel Health has obtained six FDA 510(k) clearances, monitored more than 16,000 individuals, and launched in more than 20 countries worldwide with its ICU-grade wearable sensors for maternal neonatal health.

From Rice University - Open to the Public

Event details

item.title: SynthX "Unlocking the Clinical Potential of Chemical Innovation" Workshop
item.description:
Tuesday, September 8, 2026 at 5 PM

Location: BioScience Research Collaborative

By harnessing the expertise and resources of various groups within the TMC, this long-term series aims to bridge the gap between biomedical innovations and clinical applications, offering valuable insights to researchers from Rice and the TMC regarding biomedical challenges and potential solutions. Faculty, postdocs, students, and other researchers are invited to attend. Lunch is provided. — Prof. Lingyan Shi and her team are developing and applying new ultrafast laser scanning multimodal imaging and spectroscopic technologies, such as stimulated Raman scattering (SRS), DO-SRS, second harmonic generation (SHG), Fluorescence Lifetime Imaigng (FLIM), and multiphoton fluorescence (MPF) microscopy, for visualizing molecular events and metabolic dynamics in situ at subcellular resolution for studying aging and diseases. Prof. Shi and her team discovered "Golden Window" (from 1550nm to 1870nm) wavelength for deep optical tissue imaging (Shi et al., 2016), established PRM (Zhang et al., 2024) SRS and A-POD (Jang et al., 2023) super resolution multi-molecular imaging platform. Shi Lab is developing and applying bioorthogonal SRS imaging methods for dissecting complex cellular machinery in living organisms during aging processes and disease progression, offering powerful tools potentially for diagnosis, assessing therapeutic efficacy/resistance, as well as for mechanistic understanding of scientific fundamentals in neurodegenerative diseases, cancer, drug discovery, and aging processes.

From Rice University - Open to the Public

item.title: 2026 Study Abroad Fair
item.description:
Friday, September 11, 2026 at 4 PM

Location: Grand Hall

The Rice Office of Study Abroad welcomes all Rice undergraduate students to our annual Study Abroad Fair! Please join us on Friday, September 11, 2026 from 11am to 2pmat Grand Hall (inside the RMC)! The fair is designed to give students the opportunity to meet face-to-face with our exchange and direct enrollment institutions, as well as select third-party provider representatives. In addition to study abroad program representatives, expect to see a handful of exchange students and study abroad alumni, who will be there to share their experiences. All Rice Office of Study Abroad staff members will also be present to share information and answer questions. Come ask questions and hear about the many study abroad opportunities that are available through our office!

From Rice University - Open to the Public

item.title: Demon Slayer Mini Con
item.description:
Saturday, September 19, 2026 at 10 AM

🔥 Calling all Demon Slayer Corps members, Hashira-in-training, and Tanjiro fans**!** 🔥 From Mizunoto beginners to seasoned Hashira, masters of Sun Breathing to the loyal members of the Zenitsu-squad, Demon Slayer Mini Con is your next mission! CCS Houston is hosting a one-day Total Concentration event for every slayer who lives for the Hinokami Kagura, the intensity of the Upper Rank battles, and the bond between friends. Join the Corps for a day packed with Nichirin-grade marketplace finds, Cosplay haoris, and Blood Demon Art energy, all in a fan-first space where no one is left in the Final Selection alone. Steel your heart, grab your Kasugai Crow, and let’s set our hearts ablaze together ⚔️✨   🗓️ Date & Time: Saturday, Sept 19, 2026 | 10AM – 5PM 📍 Location: 9700 Bissonnet St, #1500, Houston, TX 77036 🎟️ FREE Entry + FREE Parking   👘 Demon Slayer Cosplay Showcase + Demon Face-Off (2 PM) Prepare for a Hashira-level showdown in a dramatic pose-off against our “demon.” The crowd’s roar will determine who survives the night and claims victory.   🎴 Nichirin Sword Pose-Off (3PM) Even without a uniform, your spirit can burn bright! Grab a Nichirin blade, take the stage, and unleash your most iconic Breathing Style pose for a chance to win a prize.   🎮 Demon Slayer: Hinokami Chronicles Free-Play Zone (All Day) Jump into the action in our gaming area! Join free play sessions on the Demon Slayer: The Hinokami Chronicles video game and challenge fellow slayers to a dueling trial.   Demon Slayer Trivia (3:30 PM) Only those with the sharpest focus will survive this mental trial and earn their rank as a true scholar of the series.   🎨 Nezuko’s Box DIY Craft Station (All Day) Channel your inner Urokodaki to construct a miniature wooden-style carrier using paper, charms, and markers. Open to all ages!   🔥 Demon Slayer Artist Alley Wander through a marketplace filled with hand-forged treasures and fandom collectibles, including custom Nichirin charms, Demon Slayer-inspired prints, and talismans for the next mission, our Artist Alley features the most talented creators in the Corps. 🔥 Want to create something special for Demon Slayer fans? 🔗 Host activities, workshops, or panels → https://forms.gle/q2nCKsZ6FEVZBgqW9 🔗 Volunteer Sign-Up → https://forms.gle/15zMGTqZPSGKjGdy6 🔗 Vendor Applications → https://tinyurl.com/HoustonVendors

From CCS Houston

item.title: NIS Fall 2026 Poster Session
item.description:
Tuesday, September 22, 2026 at 5 PM

Location: Space Science Building

For non-Rice attendees, the nearest visitor lot is the North Annex Lot (Alumni@Campanile). Enter campus at entrance 20 (Kent@Rice), and turn right at the stop sign into the lot. Parking is credit/debit card only and costs $1 every 10 minutes for a daily maximum of $12. Space Science is not the corner building, but the building next to it with a loading dock. The elevator and stairs are to the left of the dock. Once on the third floor, turn right, down the hall, then left. Room 300 will be on the left. If you want to volunteer to present, please email us at nis@rice.edu (mailto:nis@rice.edu).

From Rice University - Open to the Public

item.title: Distinguished Lecture with Ken Goldberg
item.description:
Thursday, September 24, 2026 at 9 PM

Location: Duncan Hall

RSVP to Attend (https://k2i.wufoo.com/forms/distinguished-lecture-with-ken-goldberg/): The distinguished lecture is from 4:00-5:00 PM, followed by a social from 5:00-6:00 PMSpeaker: Ken Goldberg, PhD (https://goldberg.berkeley.edu/) — William S. Floyd Distinguished Professor of Engineering at UC Berkeley and President of the Robot Learning Foundation Talk Title: TBD When: Thursday, September 24, 2026 @ 4:00-6:00 PM Central Where: Auditorium, Duncan Hall, Rice University (6100 Main St., Houston, TX 77005) Parking: Paid parking will be available in Founder's Court Lot or the Entrance 3 (COB) Garage. Visit the Rice University Campus Maps webpage (https://www.rice.edu/campus-maps) for directions. Organizers: Ken Kennedy Institute (https://kenkennedy.rice.edu/)Speaker Bio Ken Goldberg is William S. Floyd Distinguished Professor of Engineering at UC Berkeley, President of the international Robot Learning Foundation, IEEE Fellow, co-founder and Editor-in-Chief emeritus of the IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering (T-ASE), and Co-Founder/Chief Scientist of Ambi Robotics. Ken leads research in robotics and automation: grasping, manipulation, and learning for applications in warehouses, homes, agriculture, and robot-assisted surgery. He is Distinguished Professor of IEOR, EECS, and Art Practice. Ken has published 12 US patents and over 450 refereed papers. He has presented over 600 invited lectures to academic and corporate audiences.

From Rice University - Open to the Public

item.title: Texas Colloquium on Distributed Learning (TL;DR 2026)
item.description:
Thursday, October 8, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: O'Connor Building for Engineering & Science

Date: Thursday & Friday, October 8-9, 2026About:The workshop onFuture-proof Distributed/Federated Learningwill take place October 8-9, 2026, in Houston, USA, at Rice University. To attend both days, we recommend arriving to Houston on the evening of October 7. We expect around 100 participants, including domestic and international researchers working in distributed learning, multi-agent systems, federated learning, optimization, large-scale AI systems, and responsible agentic AI. Read more about the the speakers and itinerary on the event website (https://sites.google.com/rice.edu/tldr2026/)! Aim of the Workshop: The purpose of this workshop is to gather leading researchers on Federated Learning, Distributed Computing and Large-Scale ML at Rice University; the workshop will discuss and focus on efficient distributed learning algorithms on how to train such large models using less costly/specialized computing platforms. Topics of interest include, but are not limited to, Systems and infrastructure, Fairness, Hardware for on-device distributed learning, Privacy concerns, Optimization advances, Theoretical contributions, and Applications of distributed learning.Support: The National Science Foundation, the Rice University Creative Venture Fund, the Rice University Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, the Rice University Department of Mechanical Engineering, the Rice University Department of Computer Science, and the Ken Kennedy Institute, and the CORE-AI OWLS Cluster.

From Rice University - Open to the Public

item.title: Distinguished Lecture with Francesco Bullo
item.description:
Thursday, October 8, 2026 at 9 PM

Location: Duncan Hall

RSVP to Attend (https://k2i.wufoo.com/forms/distinguished-lecture-with-francesco-bullo/): The distinguished lecture is from 4:00-5:00 PM, followed by a social from 5:00-6:00 PMSpeaker: Professor Francesco Bullo (https://fbullo.github.io/)— Professor of Mechanical Engineering at UC Santa Barbara Talk Title: TBD When: Thursday, October 8, 2026 @ 4:00-6:00 PM Central Where: Auditorium, Duncan Hall, Rice University (6100 Main St., Houston, TX 77005) Parking: Paid parking will be available in Founder's Court Lot or the Entrance 3 (COB) Garage. Visit the Rice University Campus Maps webpage (https://www.rice.edu/campus-maps) for directions. Organizers: Ken Kennedy Institute (https://kenkennedy.rice.edu/)Speaker Bio Francesco Bullo is Distinguished Professor and Mosher Endowed Chair of Mechanical Engineering at the University of California, Santa Barbara, CA, USA. He received the Laurea degree in Electrical Engineering from the University of Padova, Italy, in 1994, and the Ph.D. degree in Control and Dynamical Systems from the California Institute of Technology, Pasadena, CA, in 1998. His research interests include contraction theory, network systems, neural networks, and mathematical neuroscience. He is the author or coauthor of Geometric Control of Mechanical Systems (Springer, 2004), Distributed Control of Robotic Networks (Princeton, 2009), Lectures on Network Systems (KDP, v1.7, 2024), and Contraction Theory for Dynamical Systems (KDP, v1.3 2026). He served as IEEE CSS President and SIAG CST Chair. He is a Fellow of ASME, IEEE, IFAC, NetSci, and SIAM

From Rice University - Open to the Public

item.description:
Tuesday, October 20, 2026 at 5 PM

Location: Space Science Building

For non-Rice attendees, the nearest visitor lot is the North Annex Lot (Alumni@Campanile). Enter campus at entrance 20 (Kent@Rice), and turn right at the stop sign into the lot. Parking is credit/debit card only and costs $1 every 10 minutes for a daily maximum of $12. Space Science is not the corner building, but the building next to it with a loading dock. The elevator and stairs are to the left of the dock. Once on the third floor, turn right, down the hall, then left. Room 300 will be on the left. If you want to volunteer to present, please email us at nis@rice.edu (mailto:nis@rice.edu).

item.title: Engineers in the C-Suite: Leslie Duke, Chair and CEO of Burns & McDonnell
item.description:
Thursday, October 22, 2026 at 11 PM

Location: Duncan Hall

ABOUT THE SPEAKERA proven leader in the workplace and industry, Leslie Duke paved the way for countless employee-owners, community members and students through her mentorship and leadership. A registered Professional Engineer with a B.S. in Civil Engineering, Leslie has spent her career strengthening and supporting the communities in her home state of Texas and across the U.S.  She became Burns & McDonnell's eighth CEO in 2024. An entrepreneur at heart, Leslie joined the company in the late 1990s as an engineer in the Houston office's transmission and distribution services group, inspired by the employee ownership model.  Since then, she has held numerous leadership positions and has helped establish six offices. She helped expand the firm's customer base from one local Houston client to more than 650 local, regional and national clients in the industrial, refining, chemical, manufacturing and power utility markets.  During her tenure, the business delivered over $5 billion in engineering, procurement, construction, environmental and consulting services from the region.   __  ABOUT THE ENGINEERS IN THE C-SUITE SPEAKER SERIES Hosted by the Rice Center for Engineering Leadership (RCEL) (https://www.rcelconnect.org/), Engineers in the C-Suite features executives and thought leaders from technology organizations who uniquely employ or depend on engineers.  These accomplished leaders share experiences in navigating change and how engineers can equip themselves to lead the way.  Through this series, learn about how products and workplaces are being disruptively transformed through technological advancements, and how engineers are expected to not just adapt to but lead this transformation. Previous Engineers in the C-Suite speakers include:Mark Lashier, Chairman and CEO, Phillips 66 (Fall 2025) (video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9Oql-Yp92lg&t=19s (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9Oql-Yp92lg&t=19s)) Sandra Johnson, CEO of SKJ Visioneering and former CTO of IBM Africa (Fall 2024) (video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wJlT3xvHhIk) (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wJlT3xvHhIk))Antonio Neri, CEO of Hewlett Packard Enterprise (Spring 2024) (video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yBwYQvrfM14&t=4s) (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yBwYQvrfM14&t=4s))Jim Whitehurst, former CEO of Red Hat and President of IBM (Fall 2023) (video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ty2M4Dw9pcg) (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ty2M4Dw9pcg))  REGISTRATION Check back soon for RSVP link!

From Rice University - Open to the Public

item.title: School of Humanities and Arts Campbell Lecture with Ben Rhodes
item.description:
Wednesday, November 18, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Susan and Fayez Sarofim Hall

Ben Rhodes '00, who double majored at Rice in English and Political Science, is the author of the New York Times bestsellers "After the Fall: The Rise of Authoritarianism in the World We've Made" and "The World As It Is: A Memoir of the Obama White House." He is co-host of Pod Save the World; a contributor for MSNBC; a senior advisor to former President Barack Obama; and chair of National Security Action. From 2009-2017, he served as a speechwriter and Deputy National Security Advisor to President Obama. In his latest book, "All We Say: The Battle for American Identity: A History in 15 Speeches," Rhodes tells the story of 15 speeches that have shaped and reflected the argument Americans have been having from our founding to the intense divisions of our time.The Campbell Lecture Series is organized by the School of Humanities and Arts Dean's Office, with generous support from the Campbell Foundation.

From Rice University - Open to the Public

Event details

item.title: 2027 Energy HPC & AI Conference
item.description:
Tuesday, February 23, 2027 at 10 AM

Location: BioScience Research Collaborative

Conference Details: The 20th annual Energy HPC & AI Conference will be hosted by the Ken Kennedy Institute at Rice University. The conference convenes experts from the energy industry, academia, and national labs to share breakthroughs for HPC and AI technologies in improving performance and scalability. The conference takes place in Houston, TX at Rice University's BRC with plenary speaker sessions, a technical talk program, birds of a feather sessions, networking receptions, poster presentations, and add-on workshops.Conference Date: February 23-25, 2027Location: BioScience Research Collaborative (BRC) Rice University 6500 Main St, Houston, TX 77030 Houston, TXWebsite: Visit energyhpc-ai.rice.edu (http://energyhpc-ai.rice.edu) for program updatesParticipation:Call for Abstracts (https://kenkennedy.rice.edu/energyhpc-ai-participation): Due December 2 Call for Posters (https://kenkennedy.rice.edu/aihealthconference-participation): Due December 9 Sponsorship Opening Soon! (https://kenkennedy.rice.edu/energyhpc-ai-sponsorship)

From Rice University - Open to the Public

Event details

item.title: University Awards Ceremony and Reception 2027
item.description:
Tuesday, April 27, 2027 at 9 PM

The entire campus community is invited to join us as we celebrate teaching at Rice and—in particular—those instructors who care very deeply about student learning. There will be a wine and appetizer reception following the ceremony. All students, faculty, alumni, and staff are encouraged to attend! Please RSVP here: https://bit.ly/cte_RSVP (https://bit.ly/cte_RSVP).Awards To Be PresentedGeorge R. Brown Prize for Excellence in Teaching George R. Brown Awards for Superior Teaching Nicolas Salgo Outstanding Teaching Award Trenton W. Wann Award for Excellence in Teaching and Mentorship Charles W. Duncan, Jr. Achievement Award for Outstanding Faculty Sophia Meyer Farb Prize for Teaching (Phi Beta Kappa Teaching Award) Exceptional Scholar Award for Sustained Excellence Jack Gill Award for Innovation and Entrepreneurship Excellence Marjorie Corcoran Award Provost's Award for Outstanding Faculty Achievement Provost's Award for Outstanding Early-Career Achievement Provost's Award for Outstanding Doctoral Advisor Presidential Award for Mentoring Rice University Faculty Award for Excellence in Research, Teaching, and Service Rice University Faculty Award for Excellence in Professional Service and Leadership Rice University Faculty Award for Excellence in University Service and Leadership Sarah A. Burnett Teaching Prize in the Social Sciences Sarofim Professor for Distinguished Teaching in the Humanities Award for Outstanding Teaching in the School of Natural Sciences Award for Excellence in Teaching in the School of Engineering Graduate Liberal Studies John Freeman Awards Graduate Teaching Awards Student-Taught Course Teaching Awards

From Rice University - Open to the Public
